description: Iconic Texas evergreen oak providing year-round shade and structure. Produces acorns for wildlife. Long-lived anchor species.
Drought-tolerant deciduous shade tree. Drops leaves late fall providing mulch. Excellent for hot, dry sites.
Texas state tree producing delicious nuts. Needs deep soil and regular water. Provides heavy shade and leaf mulch.
Beautiful spring blooms followed by edible plums. Excellent wildlife tree. Thrives in diverse conditions.
Smaller oak with sweet acorns edible for humans and wildlife. Alkaline soil tolerant. Fast-growing for an oak.
Native persimmon with sweet fall fruit after frost. Beautiful orange fall color. Great wildlife value.
Native understory tree producing tropical-flavored fruit. Largest edible fruit native to North America. Thrives in shade and forms colonies.
toxicityWarning: Seeds and skin are toxic. Only eat ripe fruit pulp.

description: Fast-growing shade tree with fragrant purple flowers. Naturalized in Texas. Provides quick shade but can be invasive. Birds spread seeds.
toxicityWarning: All parts highly toxic to humans and livestock. Berries can be fatal if eaten.

description: Stunning pink spring blooms before leaves emerge. Edible flowers. Nitrogen-fixing legume excellent for building soil.
Fragrant pink flowers and unique black seed pods. Nitrogen-fixing legume. Drought-tolerant once established.
Early spring pink flowers appear before leaves. Thrives in shade and rocky soil. Beautiful ornamental.
White spring flowers, edible blue-black berries in fall. Glossy leaves turn burgundy. Great wildlife shrub.
Evergreen with red berries. Leaves make caffeinated tea. Extremely drought-tolerant. Birds love the berries.
Deciduous holly with brilliant red berries in winter. Excellent wildlife value. Tolerates wet and dry conditions.
Mediterranean adapted tree with sweet figs twice per year. Easy to grow. Tolerates alkaline soil and heat.
Evergreen with edible red berries for jams. Extremely drought-tolerant. Spiny leaves deter browsing.
Orange tubular flowers loved by hummingbirds. Blooms spring to fall. Drought-tolerant once established.
Low-growing groundcover shrub. Aromatic leaves. Red berries make tart 'sumac-ade'. Beautiful fall color.
Silvery evergreen foliage with purple flowers after rain. Extremely drought-tolerant. Excellent for dry sites.
Red tubular flowers attract hummingbirds. Edible flowers and fruit. Thrives in shade and spreads readily.
Mediterranean herb thriving in Texas heat. Evergreen with edible leaves and flowers. Excellent culinary and ornamental.
Heat-loving shrub with stunning orange flowers and nutritious fruit. Tolerates alkaline soil and drought.
Stunning evergreen with fragrant purple grape-scented flowers in spring. Extremely drought-tolerant. Slow-growing but worth the wait.
toxicityWarning: HIGHLY TOXIC. All parts poisonous, especially seeds. Can be fatal if ingested. Keep away from children and pets.

description: Native groundcover with tiny yellow flowers. Spreads readily in shade. Excellent living mulch and lawn alternative.
Low-growing native groundcover. White flowers loved by butterflies. Tolerates foot traffic. Great lawn substitute.
Tall aromatic grass for culinary use. Makes excellent tea. Clumping habit, drought-tolerant once established.
Mediterranean herb thriving in Texas. Spreads to form groundcover. Edible leaves and flowers attract pollinators.
Anise-flavored herb, tarragon substitute. Yellow flowers in fall. Self-seeds readily. Excellent pollinator plant.
Ornamental native grass with beautiful seed heads. Thrives in shade. Self-seeds to create groundcover.
Native grass with pink-purple plumes in fall. Clumping habit. Excellent for erosion control and wildlife.
Dynamic accumulator mining deep nutrients. Leaves make excellent mulch and compost activator. Purple flowers for pollinators.
Native sedge forming dense groundcover. Excellent lawn substitute for shade. Tolerates foot traffic.
Low-growing aromatic groundcover. Edible leaves, pink flowers attract bees. Tolerates light foot traffic.
Nitrogen-fixing groundcover. Tolerates mowing and foot traffic. Flowers attract pollinators. Enriches soil.
Stunning purple foliage groundcover. Thrives in heat and drought. Pink flowers in summer. Spreads readily.
Evergreen groundcover forming dense mat. Tolerates deep shade. Low maintenance once established.
Native vine with tubular red flowers loved by hummingbirds. Non-invasive. Evergreen in mild winters.
Stunning purple flowers produce edible passion fruit. Host plant for Gulf Fritillary butterflies. Spreads by runners.
Native grape producing large sweet fruit. Extremely disease-resistant. Needs strong support structure.
Evergreen vine with fragrant yellow flowers in spring. Fast-growing. All parts toxic but beautiful ornamental.
Host plant for Pipevine Swallowtail butterflies. Unique purple flowers. Herbaceous vine dies back in winter.
Vigorous vine producing edible tubers. Leaves also edible. Spreads to suppress weeds. Annual in most climates.
Native sunflower producing edible tubers. Very vigorous. Yellow flowers in fall. Can be invasive.
Essential culinary crop. Plant in fall for spring harvest. Companion plant deters many pests. Stores well.
Tropical rhizome for culinary use. Grows well in shade. Harvest after frost kills foliage. Stores fresh or dried.
Pungent root for culinary use. Very vigorous and can be invasive. Harvest roots in fall. Regrows from pieces.
Purple coneflower with immune-boosting medicinal properties. Excellent pollinator plant. Drought-tolerant once established.
Medicinal herb for wound healing and fever reduction. White flowers attract beneficial insects. Spreads to form groundcover.
toxicityWarning: Can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals. Avoid during pregnancy.

description: Calming medicinal herb with lemon-scented leaves. Makes excellent tea. Self-seeds readily. Attracts pollinators.
Traditional migraine remedy with daisy-like flowers. Self-seeds prolifically. Excellent insectary plant.
toxicityWarning: Can cause mouth ulcers if chewed fresh. Avoid during pregnancy.

description: Bright orange flowers for skin healing. Edible petals. Self-seeds. Blooms cool season in Texas.
Sacred Ayurvedic herb for stress relief. Spicy aromatic leaves. Purple flowers attract pollinators. Adapts to heat.
Essential garden vegetable. Many varieties adapted to Texas heat. Plant spring and fall. Needs support structure.
toxicityWarning: Leaves and green parts are toxic. Only eat ripe fruit.

description: Heat-loving annual thriving in Texas summers. Sweet bells to hot jalapeños. Excellent container plant.
Heat-loving summer vegetable thriving in Texas. Beautiful hibiscus-like flowers. Highly productive.
Native wildflower with bright yellow petals. Blooms summer to fall. Self-seeds. Essential pollinator plant.
Texas state flower. Nitrogen-fixing legume. Blue spikes in spring. Self-seeds. Must-have for Texas gardens.
toxicityWarning: All parts toxic if ingested in large quantities.

description: Essential monarch butterfly host plant. Orange flowers. Deep taproot for drought tolerance. Critical for pollinators.
toxicityWarning: Toxic if ingested. Sap can irritate skin.

description: Native yellow daisy blooming most of the year. Spreads to form groundcover. Excellent pollinator magnet.
Native perennial with red tubular flowers. Hummingbird favorite. Blooms spring to fall. Extremely drought-tolerant.
Classic European grape for eating fresh. Needs hot dry summers. Requires pruning and strong support.
Native trailing blackberry. Sweet berries in late spring. Spreads aggressively via runners. Thorny but delicious.
Medicinal and edible mushroom growing on hardwood logs. Cascading white tendrils. Cognitive health benefits. Inoculate oak logs.
Easy-to-grow edible mushroom on straw or hardwood logs. Fast colonizer. Thrives in Texas humidity. Multiple flushes per year.
Premium culinary mushroom on oak logs. Requires shaded location and consistent moisture. Produces for 3-5 years.
Garden giant mushroom growing in wood chip mulch. Burgundy cap. Easy to establish in mulch paths. Self-propagates.
Fast-growing annual providing quick temporary shade for understory plants. Edible seeds. Attracts pollinators and birds. Can be planted as makeshift shade.
Essential cool-season vegetable. Plant bulbs in fall for spring harvest. Stores well. Companion plant deters many pests.
Vigorous vine producing prolific summer fruit. Needs trellis or support. Plant after last frost. Harvest frequently.
Large-leafed vine providing living mulch. Summer and winter varieties. Zucchini, yellow squash, acorn, butternut. Sprawling habit.
Nitrogen-fixing legume. Bush or pole varieties. Plant spring and fall in Texas. Improves soil while producing food.
toxicityWarning: Raw beans contain toxins. Must be cooked thoroughly.

description: Cool-season leafy green. Plant fall through spring in Texas. Bolt-resistant varieties for heat. Successive plantings for continuous harvest.
Cool-season root vegetable. Plant fall in Texas. Needs loose, deep soil. Harvest when shoulders push up above soil.
Fast-growing cool-season root. Ready in 25-30 days. Excellent for breaking up compacted soil. Plant fall through spring.
Nutrient-dense cool-season green. Plant fall in Texas. Bolts quickly in heat. Excellent in partial shade.
Hardy cool-season green surviving Texas winters. Improved flavor after frost. Lacinato and curly varieties. Very productive.
Heat-tolerant leafy green. Beautiful rainbow stems. Thrives spring and fall. More heat-resistant than spinach.
Heat-loving summer vegetable. Purple or white fruits. Needs long warm season. Excellent in Texas heat.
toxicityWarning: Leaves and green parts toxic. Only eat ripe fruit.

description: Sweet summer fruit. Cantaloupe, honeydew varieties. Sprawling vine needs space. Thrives in Texas heat.
Classic summer fruit. Needs lots of space and heat. Small 'icebox' and large 'picnic' varieties. Texas favorite.
Evergreen with red berries. Leaves make caffeinated tea. Extremely drought-tolerant. Birds love the berries. Already listed in Understory section.
Showy summer blooms in pink, purple, white, red. Beautiful exfoliating bark. Drought-tolerant once established. Multi-trunk small tree.
Evergreen hedge plant with white fragrant flowers. Can be invasive. Birds spread seeds. Fast-growing screen.
toxicityWarning: Berries toxic if ingested. Can cause nausea and digestive upset.

description: Large heart-shaped leaves provide dense shade. White orchid-like flowers. Long bean pods. Fast-growing shade tree.
Stunning purple foliage groundcover. Thrives in heat and drought. Pink flowers in summer. Spreads readily. Already listed in Groundcover section.
Native thorny vine with edible young shoots. Medicinal roots. Aggressive spreader via rhizomes. Wildlife food and cover.
toxicityWarning: Very thorny. Handle with care to avoid injury.

description: Native deciduous oak with brilliant red fall color. Smaller than live oak. Acorns for wildlife. Limestone soil tolerant.
Epiphytic jungle cactus with zigzag leaves. White night-blooming flowers. Grows in trees or hanging baskets. Shade-loving succulent.
Drought-tolerant deciduous shade tree. Drops leaves late fall providing mulch. Excellent for hot, dry sites. Already listed in Canopy section.
